title: Is it ethical to use AI? Make AI Boring Again
body:
Is it ethical to use AI? Make AI Boring Again

On externalities and engagement, the problem with purity politics, and why we need to make AI boring again. By Charity Majors, June 24, 2026.

AI is just technology. Some technologies are more damaging than others, but we always discover risks before we know how to govern them. There is always a gap while we try to catch up. That gap is not proof that AI is evil. It is proof that we have work to do.

The fact that we have not solved the problems yet is not an argument to disconnect. It is an argument to engage, especially if you work in technology and already have an arsenal of relevant skills. You do not learn to govern a tool by refusing to touch it. You learn by using it and understanding well enough to critique it, shape it, contribute to it, and set boundaries around it. You learn how to make it boring.

There are two buckets of harms associated with AI: harms done in the creation of AI, such as training without permission or compensation, and labor exploitation in data labeling. And harms enabled by the use of AI, such as revenge porn, the ouroboros of truthiness, energy and water usage.

Where I diverge from many is that I don't think awareness of these harms leads inexorably to the conclusion that I should not use AI. I think the moral valence points in the other direction, especially for those of us in tech. I think we have a moral responsibility to engage, become experts, become people worth listening to.

On the legal question of training data: was it legal? It's effectively Schrodinger's Law, we won't find out until the Supreme Court weighs in. But even if it turns out to have been legal, was it right? Not in my book.

The list of harms people are currently experiencing is long. From everyday irritants like getting five pages of slop instead of three crisp bullet points, to deadly serious concerns about skill atrophy, lack of accountability, and the loss of truth itself. Most of these are not novel to AI, they were problems before AI came around.

My politics are not concerned with purity. I believe in interdependence. I believe we are inescapably entwined and entangled with one another. The way you show care is by showing up. The way you make the world a better place is by getting down in the muck and building it. The way you drive change is you engage. Yes, we are all complicit. But what are you going to do with that feeling of conviction? Will you channel your discomfort into solidarity and action, or try to ease your conscience by removing yourself from the system?

You can't fight fundamentalism with fundamentalism. The pursuit of purity is the animating force behind every fundamentalism. While fundamentalism is an emotionally satisfying response, I do not think it is an effective response.

What should we do? I think that anyone who works in tech should be actively learning everything they can about AI. I think every workplace that uses AI should be actively talking about the ways AI is changing how we work together. Not just to catalogue harms, but to actively experiment with ways of pushing back on them.

Start where you live. Start at work. Are you getting frustrated with AI slop and undisciplined use of AI tooling? These gripes are worth airing as a way of figuring out better ways of interacting and working together.

The answer to fear-driven rage is boring, disciplined, collective work. The answer to fundamentalism is not more fundamentalism. The goal is not to make AI disappear. It's to make its use disciplined, social and accountable. Let's make AI boring again.
